content_markdown: When $1800\text{ J}$ of energy is supplied to a mass $m$ of liquid in a container, the temperature of the liquid and the container changes by $10\text{ K}$. When the mass of the liquid is doubled to $2m$, $3000\text{ J}$ of energy is required to change the temperature of the liquid and container by $10\text{ K}$. What is the specific heat capacity of the liquid in $\text{J kg}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: Let $C$ be the heat capacity of the container and $c$ be the specific heat capacity of the liquid. For the first case: $1800 = (mc + C) \times 10$. For the second case: $3000 = (2mc + C) \times 10$. Dividing by 10 gives $180 = mc + C$ and $300 = 2mc + C$. Subtracting the first equation from the second yields $120 = mc$, so $c = \frac{120}{m}$.
